sections.label: Let us do these activities before we read

questions.question_html: We know that frogs croak. Did you know that ravens also croak?
questions.solution_html: Yes, I know this fact about Ravens, as crows also croak.

questions.question_html:
Which animal is shown to be cunning in stories? Circle the correct answer.

  1. tiger
  2. fox
  3. bear
Mr Raven is a ________. (bird/frog)
The name of the fox is ________. (Raven/Reynard)
Mr Raven had ________ in his beak. (a piece of food/ a bunch of leaves)
The Fox calls the Raven a __________ bird. (foolish/good-looking)
The Fox asks the Raven to ________. (dance/sing)
The Raven and the Fox live in the ________. (trees/woods)
The Raven ________ that he croaked. (remembered/forgot)
The Raven opened its beak to ________. (eat/sing)
The ________ fell down. (food/Raven)
The Fox ________ at the Raven. (laughed/looked)
It is not ________ to be too proud of oneself. (wise/unwise)
The Raven learnt a ________. (lesson/teaching)

Read the poem again and match the words in Column A with their meanings in Column B. Check your answers with your teacher and your classmates.

Column A Column B
(a) perched (i) a small piece of food
(b) morsel (ii) looked with interest at something
(c) seek (iii) feeling that you are better than others
(d) pride (iv) sat on a branch
(e) eyed (v) to look for something
(f) limb (vi) a smaller area of forest with similar kind of trees
(g) woods (vii) branch of a tree
Look at the pictures and number them in the order that they happen in the poem.

Read the following lines and answer the questions that follow.
Sir Raven, you are a handsome bird.
Such feathers! If you would only sing,
The birds of these woods would call you King.

  1. ‘Such feathers’ refer to ________ feathers.
    1. shiny and beautiful
    2. black and dull
    3. grey and long
    4. short and unattractive
  2. Why would the birds of the woods call the Raven King?
  3. Why does the Fox address the Raven as Sir?
questions.solution_html:
1. (a) shiny and beautiful
  2. The birds would call the Raven as ‘King’ if he sang.
  3. The fox addressed the Raven as ‘Sir’ out of flattery only.

questions.question_html:
Read the following lines and answer the questions that follow.
The Raven, who did not see the joke,
Forgot that his voice was just a croak.
He opened his beak, in his foolish pride-

  1. Why did the Raven open his beak?
  2. Complete the following with one word from the given lines.
    Fox : clever :: Raven : ________
questions.solution_html:
1. Raven opened his beak to sing as the Fox flattered him.
  2. Foolish

questions.question_html: Why does the Raven forget that his voice is just a croak?
questions.solution_html: Raven forgets that his voice is just a croak. It is due to his pride. He falls into a net woven by the clever Fox.

questions.question_html: How does Reynard make the Raven sing?
questions.solution_html: Reynard makes the Raven sing out of flattering only. He uses some words like ‘King’ just to flatter the Raven.

questions.question_html: Why does Reynard say that pride is not wise?
questions.solution_html: Reynard says so rightly as too much pride leads one to loss and suffering only.

questions.question_html: Give one reason why the teaching is quite a surprise. Answer to the context of the chapter The Raven and the Fox.
questions.solution_html: The teaching is quite a surprise because the Raven is not as clever as the Fox is.

questions.question_html: Imagine someone praises you too much. How would you react?
questions.solution_html: I would be alert after hearing the praise. Then I would analyze what is what and take proper steps to know the reality.

questions.question_html:
Write the rhyming words from the poem. One has been done for you.

Stanza 1 Stanza 2 Stanza 3 Stanza 4
(a) limb-him (a) word-____ (a) joke-____ (a) know-____
(b) ____ ____ (b) ____ ____ (b) ____ ____ (b) ____ ____
questions.solution_html:
Stanza 1 Stanza 2 Stanza 3 Stanza 4
(a) limb-him (a) word-bird (a) joke-croak (a) know-glow
(b) beak-seek (b) sing-king (b) pride-eyed (b) sun-wise surprise

questions.question_html:
Study the underlined words in the poem.

  1. For the Raven held in his great big beak.
    The words ‘big’ and ‘beak’ begin with the same /b/ sound.
  2. The song of sweet birds.

Now, pick another example of alliteration from Stanza 2.

Choose the correct opposites from the box given below and complete the table. There are two extra words that you will not need.

wise, happy, humility, flew, remembered, notice, dim, cried, silly
Words Opposites
1. perched  
2. forgot  
3. foolish  
4. pride  
5. laughed  
6. ignore  
7. glow  

Use any four words from the table given above to make sentences of your own.
questions.solution_html:
Words Opposites
1. perched Flew
2. forgot Remembered
3. foolish Wise
4. pride Humility
5. laughed Cried
6. ignore Notice
7. glow Dim

Sentences

  1. The crow perched on a branch.
  2. I forgot to bring my notebook.
  3. Don’t be foolish. Come to the point.
  4. Pride hath a fall.
  5. The children laughed at the joker.
  6. Don’t ignore him in any way.
  7. The stars glow at night.

questions.question_html:
You will listen to what the crow did after he lost his food. As you listen, mark the given statements as True or False. (refer to page 37 for transcript)

  1. The crow was unhappy that he lost his food. ()
  2. The crow thought that his lovely feathers made him smart. ()
  3. The crow wanted to tell his friends not to be proud. ()
questions.solution_html:
1. The crow was unhappy that he lost his food. (True)
  2. The crow thought that his lovely feathers made him smart. (False)
  3. The crow wanted to tell his friends not to be proud. (True)

questions.question_html:
Narrate the poem in the form of a story. Give a different ending to the story. You may begin like this:
This is a story about a clever fox named Reynard and Mr Raven. One day ...

Now, write the story you narrated in the form of a conversation.
(Remember to write only the actions for the Raven as he has food in his mouth and does not speak.)
You may begin like this.
Reynard: Good morning, Mr Raven! You seem to be awake so early!
(Mr Raven just nods his head)
Reynard: Oh, Mr Raven, you look ...

Origami is an art of folding objects out of paper to create both two-dimensional and three-dimensional objects.
Let us make stick puppets and enact the poem. You may take the help of the pictures given below.


Note: You may use the puppets while narrating the story.
